sections.title: What are modal verbs? (1)
sections.content: Modal verbs add a layer of meaning to the main verb: possibility, obligation, permission, ability, advice. **Main modals:** *can, could, may, might, must, should, will, would.*

sections.title: What are modal verbs? (2)
sections.content: **Form:** no *-s* in third person (*she can*, not *she cans*). Followed by **base form** (*can go*, *must see*). No *do* in questions or negatives (*Can you?* *I must not*).

sections.title: Ability and permission
sections.content:
**Can** — ability: *I can swim.* Permission: *You can leave early.*
**Could** — past ability: *I could run fast when I was young.* Polite request: *Could you help me?*
**May** — formal permission: *May I sit here?*

sections.title: Possibility
sections.content:
**May / might** — possibility: *It may rain. She might be late.* (*might* = slightly less certain)
**Could** — also possible: *He could be right.*
These all mean *perhaps* — the action is not certain.

sections.title: Obligation and advice (1)
sections.content:
**Must** — strong obligation or rule: *You must wear a seatbelt.* Strong deduction: *She must be at home* (I'm sure).
**Should** — advice, weak obligation: *You should see a doctor.*

sections.title: Obligation and advice (2)
sections.content: **Ought to** — similar to *should*, often more formal: *You ought to apologise.* Form: *ought to* + base verb.

sections.title: Must vs have to (1)
sections.content: Both express obligation, but: **must** = the speaker decides (personal obligation, strong rule). **Have to** = external obligation (rules, boss, law): *I have to wear a uniform.*

sections.title: Must vs have to (2)
sections.content: **Have to** behaves like a normal verb: *She has to go. Do you have to? I don't have to.* Use **be able to** when you need a form that *can* doesn't have: *She will be able to come.*

sections.title: Typical errors (1)
sections.content:
**Wrong:** *She cans swim.* **Right:** *She can swim* (no *-s*).
**Wrong:** *You must to go.* **Right:** *You must go* (no *to* after modals).

sections.title: Typical errors (2)
sections.content:
**Wrong:** *Do you can help?* **Right:** *Can you help?* (no *do* with modals).
**Wrong:** *I must go to work yesterday.* **Right:** *I had to go* (*must* has no past form — use *had to*).

sections.title: Reference
sections.content: For full modal verb lists and examples, see **Reference** → **Modal Verbs**.

questions.question: What is **modality** in grammar?

options.a: The time of the action (past, present, future)
options.b: The way we express possibility, necessity, permission, advice, etc.
options.c: The person (I, you, he/she) of the subject

questions.explanation: **Modality** is how we express meanings like possibility, necessity, permission, ability, obligation, or advice – the 'layer' of meaning added by modals and semi-modals.

questions.question: Which sentence uses a **semi-modal**?

options.b: You ought to see a doctor.

questions.explanation: **Ought to** is a semi-modal (followed by **to** + base verb). **Can**, **must**, and **might** are core modals (no *to*).

questions.question: 'You ____ apologise.' (strong advice, formal) Which fits best?
questions.explanation: **Ought to** expresses advice or moral obligation, often in a more formal tone. *Should* is also possible but *ought to* fits 'formal' best here.

questions.question: Which of these uses 'to' before the main verb? (e.g. ___ to go)
questions.explanation: **Ought to** always includes 'to' before the main verb: we say 'ought to go', 'ought to see'. **Must**, **should**, and **can** are followed directly by the base form (must go, should see, can go).

questions.question: For external obligation (e.g. a rule or someone else's requirement), we often use:
questions.explanation: **Have to** is commonly used for external obligation (rules, boss, law). **Must** can express the speaker's own obligation or a strong rule; **have to** is typical when the obligation comes from outside.

questions.question: Which verb do we use for **ability** when we need a form that *can* doesn't have (e.g. future)?
questions.explanation: **Be able to** is used for ability when we need another tense or structure, e.g. *She will be able to come*, *I have been able to finish*. **Can** has no future or perfect form.

gapfill.title: Practice: Choose the modal or semi-modal

questions.question: You ____ (advice) see a doctor if your headache continues. (ought to / should)
She ____ speak three languages. (ability)
questions.explanation: Ability in the present → **can** + base verb.

questions.question: We ____ wear a uniform at school. (external rule)
It ____ rain later, so take an umbrella. (possibility)
You ____ (not) tell anyone – it's a secret. (obligation not to)
questions.explanation: Obligation not to do something → **must not** or **cannot**.

questions.question: He ____ give up smoking. (advice, semi-modal)
questions.explanation: Advice with the semi-modal → **ought to** + base verb.
